narrative:
# EOD MACRO-RISK BRIEF | 2026-06-29

**Headline:** Overall risk stable at 5.2, but food-supply vulnerability escalated to High amid simultaneous China export controls and Gulf disruption.

**Level Changes:**
• **Food & fertilizer fragility** (6→7, now High): China tightened fertilizer export curbs (vs. baseline in-place status) and Hormuz/Gulf transit shifted from disrupted to cut, creating dual-source shock to global input availability.

**Watch Most Closely:**
Food & fertilizer fragility at Level 7—simultaneous export controls and chokepoint closure compress near-term planting and harvest cycles with limited substitution windows.

stories.name: Treasury basis trade (hidden leverage)

indicators.label: SOFR minus EFFR (%)
MOVE index (Treasury volatility)
SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)

stories.summary: Hedge funds run a highly leveraged arbitrage between Treasury cash bonds and futures. It is a major source of liquidity, but a spike in funding costs or Treasury volatility can force rapid unwinds, as briefly happened in March 2020 and again in 2025.

stories.name: Japan fiscal / JGB / carry trade

indicators.label: USD/JPY (yen strengthening = lower = risk)
30-year JGB yield (%)
Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)

stories.summary: Decades of near-zero rates made Japan the funding leg of a vast global carry trade. As the BoJ normalizes and JGB yields rise, fiscal financing gets harder and carry unwinds can transmit stress worldwide - the August 2024 mini-crash was a preview.

stories.name: Private credit's opaque expansion

indicators.label: US high-yield OAS (%, ≈310bps)
Direct-lending default rate (%)
Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)

stories.summary: Lending has migrated from banks to lightly regulated private-credit funds, where assets are marked infrequently and leverage is opaque. Defaults are rising from a low base; the risk is that losses surface suddenly rather than being visible in real time.

stories.name: Developing-world debt crisis

indicators.label: Broad US dollar index (FRED DTWEXBGS)
US 10y real yield (TIPS, %)
New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A strong dollar, high real rates, and heavy foreign-currency debt loads leave many developing economies vulnerable to a sudden stop. A cluster of sovereign defaults or new IMF programs would signal the slow-burn crisis turning acute.

stories.name: China's exported deflation

indicators.label: China PPI YoY (%) - more negative is worse
USD/CNY (yuan depreciation = higher = risk)
New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)

stories.summary: Weak domestic demand and industrial overcapacity are pushing Chinese producer prices into sustained deflation, which China exports through cheaper goods. It pressures global manufacturers and feeds trade tensions and tariff cycles.

stories.name: Critical minerals as a weapon

indicators.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)

stories.summary: China dominates the mining and especially the processing of rare earths and critical minerals, and has shown willingness to use export licensing as leverage. Supply chains for magnets and defense inputs have little near-term substitute.

stories.name: Stablecoins & Treasury demand

indicators.label: Largest stablecoin max peg deviation (%)
Total USD stablecoin supply ($bn)
GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Dollar stablecoins have become sizeable holders of short-term Treasuries, linking crypto plumbing to the funding market. A large redemption wave could force rapid T-bill selling; the GENIUS Act's implementation is the key regulatory variable.

stories.name: AI build-out circular financing

indicators.label: US investment-grade OAS (%) - big-issuer proxy
AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)

stories.summary: AI infrastructure spending vastly exceeds current AI revenue, increasingly financed with debt and circular vendor arrangements. If monetization disappoints or GPU-backed financing strains, the capex cycle could reverse sharply.

stories.name: CRE maturity wall / regional banks

indicators.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)

stories.summary: A wall of commercial real-estate loans - especially office - matures into higher rates and lower valuations, concentrated at regional banks. Rising delinquencies and 'extend and pretend' practices defer, rather than resolve, the losses.

stories.name: France / eurozone fragility

indicators.label: OAT-Bund 10y spread (%)
Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)

stories.summary: France combines a wide deficit, a fragmented parliament, and recurring budget standoffs, widening its spread over Germany. Political instability or a rating downgrade would test eurozone cohesion at its second-largest economy.

stories.name: Hidden consumer debt

indicators.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
US unemployment rate (%)
BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)

stories.summary: Beneath headline figures, subprime auto and buy-now-pay-later delinquencies are climbing, and the labor market is the swing factor. A rise in unemployment would convert stretched-but-current borrowers into defaults quickly.

stories.name: Gold / de-dollarization

indicators.label: Gold price (USD/oz)
USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)

stories.summary: Central banks are accumulating gold and building non-dollar settlement arrangements at the margin. It is a slow erosion rather than a sudden shift, but reserve 'weaponization' events can accelerate diversification away from the dollar.

stories.name: Insurance retreat

indicators.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
US single-family mortgage delinquency (%, FRED DRSFRMACBS)
Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)

stories.summary: Insurers are retreating from climate-exposed markets through non-renewals and exits, shifting risk onto homeowners and state backstops. Severe catastrophe seasons strain the system and feed into housing and mortgage stress.

stories.name: Pensions / demographics

indicators.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
Large public-pension funded ratio (%)

stories.summary: Aging demographics and underfunded public pensions create slow fiscal pressure, while leveraged liability-driven strategies can blow up fast - as the UK gilt/LDI crisis showed in 2022. Benefit-reform fights are the political pressure valve.

stories.name: AI power & the grid bottleneck

indicators.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)

stories.summary: Data-center electricity demand is colliding with multi-year grid interconnection queues and slow capacity additions. Whether this is an acute shortfall or eases depends on the pace of AI build-out versus new supply - forecasts diverge widely.

stories.name: Copper & the electrification deficit

indicators.label: Copper price (USD/lb, COMEX HG=F)
Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)

stories.summary: Electrification and AI infrastructure point to structural copper deficits as demand outruns new mine supply, which takes a decade to build. Price is the live signal; mine disruptions and inventory draws would confirm tightening.

stories.name: Water - the underpriced input

indicators.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)

stories.summary: Water is systematically underpriced yet essential to chip fabrication, agriculture, and power. Droughts in key hubs (Taiwan's fabs, major farming regions) and transboundary disputes can disrupt supply chains in ways markets rarely price in advance.

stories.name: Food & fertilizer fragility

indicators.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)

stories.summary: Food security depends on concentrated fertilizer supply chains - potash, phosphate, and gas-derived nitrogen - exposed to export curbs and shipping chokepoints. Curbs or input shocks feed through to prices with a lag.

stories.name: The lithography (ASML) chokepoint

indicators.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)

stories.summary: ASML is the sole maker of EUV lithography machines, with Zeiss the sole optics supplier - a single point of failure for advanced chips. Export controls, retaliation, or any disruption to this chain would ripple across the entire tech economy.

stories.name: Sea lanes & undersea cables

indicators.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
WTI crude oil (USD/bbl, CL=F)
Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)

stories.summary: A few maritime chokepoints and undersea cables carry most global trade and data. The 2026 Hormuz/Red Sea disruptions showed how quickly closures spike energy and shipping costs; cable cuts are a parallel, under-watched vulnerability.

stories.name: Clearinghouses (financial chokepoint)

indicators.label: Equity volatility (VIX)
Treasury volatility (MOVE)
Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)

stories.summary: Central counterparties now concentrate the system's risk by design, and Treasury central-clearing mandates will route more activity through them. In a volatility spike, procyclical margin calls can amplify stress - they are a systemic single point of failure.

items.label: Largest stablecoin max peg deviation (%)
items.rationale: Stablecoin.com live data (updated June 19, 2026) shows USDT trading at $0.999182, a deviation of -0.082% from $1.00, the largest among top-tier fiat-backed stablecoins; USDC was -0.017%. No significant depeg event for USDT or USDC has been reported in June 2026, though smaller/niche stablecoins (MIM, apxUSD) experienced larger deviations. [cross-check: two reads disagreed (0.082 vs 71.0); held for review]

items.label: Large public-pension funded ratio (%)
items.rationale: Milliman's Public Pension Funding Index (PPFI), which tracks the 100 largest U.S. public defined benefit plans, reported a funded ratio of 87.6% as of April 30, 2026, rebounding from 83.7% at end of March; this is corroborated by Equable Institute's most recent annual estimate of 82.5% as of December 31, 2025.

items.label: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed)
items.rationale: The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed to commercial shipping since late February 2026 following U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran; as of June 29, transit volumes remain at roughly 5% of pre-crisis levels (5 vessels vs. ~93/day normal), with IMO actively evacuating stranded seafarers and war-risk insurance at 8× pre-crisis rates. A fragile U.S.-Iran diplomatic process is underway (talks halted attacks as of June 28 per live trackers, and a June 17 MOU was signed), but the strait remains commercially non-navigable with only naval-escorted convoys moving and Iran's status still contested between its own military and foreign ministry.

items.label: Single-point concentration (0 easing / 1 high / 2 extreme)
items.rationale: Meaningful diversification is actively progressing: TSMC's Arizona Fab 1 is in profitable high-volume production (4nm), Fab 2 tool installation targets Q3 2026 for 2H 2027 mass production, a third fab is under construction, and Japan's JASM Kumamoto Fab 1 turned profitable in Q1 2026 with a second Kumamoto fab (upgraded to 3nm) approved—all part of a $52–56B 2026 capex plan targeting ~30% of leading-edge output outside Taiwan. While Taiwan still dominates current capacity, the trajectory is clear and accelerating diversification.

items.label: China fertilizer export curbs (0 lifted / 1 in place / 2 tightened)
items.rationale: China tightened fertilizer export curbs progressively in 2026: the NDRC suspended phosphate fertilizer exports through August 2026 in December 2025, then in mid-March 2026 Beijing extended bans to nitrogen-potassium blends and additional phosphate varieties, with tight urea quotas (~1.5–1.6M tonnes for June–August) still active and industry sources not expecting restrictions to lift before August. The Food Security Portal tracker confirms China 'has continued to impose restrictions on key fertilizer exports, including urea and phosphate fertilizers, through quotas and other controls.'

items.label: Hormuz/Gulf disruption to fertilizer inputs (0 clear / 1 disrupted / 2 cut)
items.rationale: As of June 29, 2026 (Day 120), the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to commercial shipping: live trackers report only ~5 vessels/day vs. ~93/day pre-crisis, with mines uncleared, war-risk insurance at 8x pre-crisis levels, and Iran's PGSA still controlling access. Sulfur and ammonia exports from the Gulf—which account for ~44% and ~20–30% of global seaborne trade respectively—remain severely disrupted, with ammonia prices up ~20% and sulfur up ~30% since the crisis began.

items.label: 30-year JGB yield (%)
items.rationale: TradingEconomics reported the Japan 30-year JGB yield at 3.88% on June 24, 2026, the most recent session data found; no intraday June 29 figure was retrievable from search snippets, but this is the latest confirmed close.

items.label: Direct-lending default rate (%)
items.rationale: Proskauer's Private Credit Default Index—tracking 697 senior-secured and unitranche U.S. loans totaling $189.2 billion in original principal—reported a direct-lending default rate of 2.73% for Q1 2026, up from 2.46% in Q4 2025 and 1.84% in Q3 2025. This is the most granular, current, and widely cited benchmark for U.S. private credit direct lending defaults, published April 27, 2026.

items.label: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems)
items.rationale: All major GENIUS Act implementing rules (OCC, FDIC, Treasury/FinCEN, OCC AML) remain at the proposed-rulemaking (NPRM) stage as of late June 2026, with final rules not yet issued and comment periods only recently closing; the Act's effective date (January 18, 2027, or 120 days after final rules) has not been triggered, and notable gaps persist—including unresolved OCC/FDIC divergences on reserve-failure consequences, an unresolved yield/rewards standoff, and law-enforcement criticism that the law lacks fraud-victim protections. No single catastrophic non-compliance failure has occurred, but the rulemaking pipeline is behind the statutory July 2026 deadline for most final rules, creating material compliance friction and uncertainty for issuers.

items.label: Large-fund redemption gates (0 none / 1 isolated / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: Multiple major private credit managers — including Apollo, Blue Owl, BlackRock (HPS), Ares, and Morgan Stanley — have all activated 5% quarterly redemption caps in Q1–Q2 2026 amid redemption requests ranging from 9% to over 40%, with a Congressional Research Service report noting the gating has generated 'widespread concerns and congressional attention.' The most recent data point (CNBC, ~June 23, 2026) shows Apollo capping its $26B Debt Solutions fund after 17% redemption requests, confirming gating remains active and widespread across large funds through mid-2026.

items.label: SEC central clearing on track (0 ok / 1 slipping / 2 delayed)
items.rationale: The SEC formally extended the original compliance deadlines by one year (to Dec. 31, 2026 for cash and June 30, 2027 for repo) in February 2025, constituting a partial/phased delay; as of April–May 2026 the SEC is actively soliciting further exemptive relief requests from SIFMA (inter-affiliate scope) and IIB (extraterritorial transactions), and Treasury TBAC documents note open implementation issues remain, signaling notable ongoing industry pushback but no full cancellation or additional postponement of the mandate.

items.label: Takaichi plan financing clarity (0 ok / 1 unclear / 2 disorderly)
items.rationale: JGB yields remain at multi-decade highs (10-year ~2.61%, 30-year ~3.88%) reflecting persistent fiscal and monetary pressure from Takaichi's expansionary stimulus plan, though the acute disorderly spike of January 2026 has partially subsided and recent auctions have cleared without reported failures. The May 2026 supplementary budget included a pledge of no extra net borrowing, providing partial market reassurance, but yields remain structurally elevated and financing uncertainty persists.

items.label: Total USD stablecoin supply ($bn)
items.rationale: As of late May 2026, CoinDesk and MacroMicro reported the total stablecoin market cap at ~$322B, and stablecoinbeat.com showed $307.5B for June 2026 (tracking a subset of 12 coins); given the upward trend and continued inflows reported by DefiLlama (~$310B tracked across all chains per eco.com), a current figure in the ~$325B range is the best estimate for end-June 2026.

items.label: Treasury-clearing rollout stress (0 smooth / 1 friction / 2 problems)
items.rationale: The SEC's May 2026 TBAC report confirms the industry has made significant strides but that 'the largest challenge from an implementation standpoint continues to be the amount of legal work and documentation' and that 'there are still open issues that need to be resolved,' including unresolved inter-affiliate exemption and extraterritorial scoping questions still out for comment as of April–May 2026. Market participants are at varying levels of readiness, and the ICI has called for a further 24-month extension, indicating meaningful friction, though clearance volumes have grown substantially and no disorderly collapse is evident.

items.label: AI capex-vs-revenue gap concern (0 closing / 1 wide / 2 widening fast)
items.rationale: Hyperscaler AI capex is projected at ~$725B in 2026 (up 77% YoY), far outpacing AI-specific revenue — e.g., Microsoft's ~$25B AI revenue versus ~$190B capex — but Q1 2026 earnings showed strong cloud revenue acceleration (Google Cloud +63% YoY, cloud margins expanding, record backlogs), leading Jefferies to call the bear thesis 'garbage'; the gap is wide but revenue is visibly catching up, not widening in a disappointing direction.

items.label: BNPL delinquency trend (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: Survey data (LendingTree, March 2026) shows 47% of BNPL users reported a late payment in the past year, up from 41% in 2025 and 34% prior, and the Richmond Fed's February 2026 brief notes 'indicative evidence from consumer surveys that late payment and delinquency behavior among BNPL users has increased since 2023.' Affirm's 30-plus-day delinquency rate was 2.8% in Q1 2026 (flat YoY), and Statista notes Affirm's delinquency rate 'increased in Q2 2026'—indicating a rising but not yet sharply-spiking trend with no major lender distress evident.

items.label: CMBS office delinquency rate (%)
items.rationale: Trepp reported the CMBS office delinquency rate surged 103 bps to an all-time high of 12.34% in January 2026, confirmed by ConnectCRE and CRE Daily citing Trepp data; a May 2026 Trepp report exists (overall rate 7.55%) but the office-specific figure for months beyond January was not retrievable.

items.label: Catastrophe-loss pressure (0 normal / 1 elevated / 2 severe season)
items.rationale: Q1 2026 global insured catastrophe losses were $20B, 26% below the 10-year average with no single event exceeding $10B (Gallagher Re, April 2026); the 2026 Atlantic hurricane season is forecast to be subdued due to El Niño, and no major wildfire or hurricane loss events have been reported as of late June 2026.

items.label: GPU-backed debt refinancing stress (0 none / 1 signs / 2 acute)
items.rationale: Multiple 2026 sources identify clear early-strain signals: CoreWeave's legacy GPU-backed debt carries ~$4.2B refinancing due in 2026 against sharply depreciated collateral (variable rate ~11%, repayments starting Jan 2026), and analysts widely flag the 'GPU debt treadmill' mismatch between GPU lifecycles and facility lifespans; however, no confirmed acute default or distressed restructuring in GPU-backed data center debt has been publicly reported as of late June 2026, with major new issuance (e.g., CoreWeave's $8.5B investment-grade deal) still clearing markets.

items.label: Insurer non-renewals / exits (0 stable / 1 rising / 2 accelerating)
items.rationale: Multiple 2026 sources confirm that non-renewals and market exits are ongoing across at least 13+ states (CA, FL, TX, CO, NC, MN, OK, NE, and others), with insurers using ZIP-code-level AI underwriting to drop policies broadly. However, the overall market is showing partial stabilization signals — AM Best revised its US homeowners outlook to 'stable' in late 2025, reinsurance costs fell ~10–25% at June 2026 renewals, and survey data shows the share of homeowners reporting cancellations/non-renewals actually declined year-over-year — indicating rising but not clearly accelerating withdrawals.

items.label: Regional-bank CRE stress (0 calm / 1 isolated / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: The FDIC confirms only 2 bank failures in 2026 (both small, firm-specific), with no systemic deposit flight or cluster of CRE-driven collapses; however, analysts characterize the 2025–2026 regional bank CRE pattern as 'individual institution events' with earnings pressure and capital strain at select CRE-concentrated lenders, consistent with isolated stress rather than calm or a systemic cluster.

items.label: Subprime auto 60+ day delinquency (%)
items.rationale: Per Fitch Ratings data cited by Wolf Street (May 19, 2026), the subprime auto ABS 60+ day delinquency rate peaked at a record 6.90% in January 2026 and edged down to 6.80% in February 2026, the most recent month reported. March data had not yet been released as of that publication.

items.label: Govt stability (0 stable / 1 fragile / 2 collapse-or-snap-election)
items.rationale: France's minority government under PM Sébastien Lecornu survived two no-confidence votes in February 2026 and passed the 2026 budget via Article 49.3, but remains structurally fragile: it governs without a parliamentary majority, depends on Socialist abstention through costly concessions, and faces persistent snap-election speculation ahead of the 2027 presidential vote.

items.label: New IMF programs / missed coupons (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: In mid-April 2026, the IMF announced it expected 'at least a dozen countries' to seek new loan programs due to Middle East conflict-driven energy price surges and supply chain disruptions, and in February 2026 the IMF approved a new $8.1B EFF program for Ukraine amid ongoing war-related debt distress. These developments indicate rising—but not yet clustered-default-level—sovereign distress consistent with a score of 1.

items.label: Pension-fund leverage event, UK-2022 type (0 none / 1 stress / 2 blowup)
items.rationale: Multiple authoritative sources (TPR, ESMA June 2026) confirm LDI funds have materially reduced leverage and raised buffers since 2022; the LDI market shrank from ~£1.5tn to ~£0.7tn by March 2025, and 'LDI arrangements remained resilient' during gilt yield volatility in early 2025 with no forced-selling episodes reported in 2026.

items.label: Rating action (0 stable / 1 negative outlook / 2 downgrade)
items.rationale: Moody's changed France's outlook to negative on October 24, 2025 (affirming Aa3), while S&P downgraded France to A+ in October 2025 with a stable outlook subsequently confirmed in May 2026, and Fitch downgraded France to A+ in September 2025. The active Moody's negative outlook is the most adverse current signal, placing the composite picture firmly at rubric level 1 (negative outlook present).

items.label: Reserve-weaponization / settlement shift (0 none / 1 talk / 2 action)
items.rationale: In 2026, multiple new non-dollar settlement initiatives are actively advancing—including BRICS Pay's operational deployment, the BRICS 'Unit' gold-backed token launch, and CBDC interoperability frameworks—driven explicitly by the weaponization of dollar reserves (e.g., the frozen $300B in Russian reserves). Additionally, in June 2026 the US is reportedly considering using Iranian frozen assets for Gulf reconstruction, signaling continued use of reserve freezes as geopolitical tools. However, no single new major sovereign reserve freeze comparable to Russia 2022 has occurred; the evidence points to accelerating talk, agreements, and incremental shifts rather than a discrete Level 2 event.

items.label: Social Security / pension reform shock (0 none / 1 debate / 2 cuts)
items.rationale: The 2026 Social Security Trustees Report (released June 9, 2026) projects the OASI trust fund will be depleted in 2032—one year earlier than prior estimates—triggering an automatic 22% benefit cut absent congressional action; CRFB, SSA, Brookings, and others are actively sounding alarms, but no concrete benefit cuts or emergency legislation have been enacted, placing this firmly in active reform debate and trust-fund warnings.

items.label: USD share of FX reserves (%, IMF COFER)
items.rationale: The IMF's official COFER Data Brief published March 27, 2026 reports the USD share of global FX reserves fell to 56.77% in 2025Q4 (from 56.93% in 2025Q3); this is the most recent quarterly release available as of June 29, 2026.

items.label: China PPI YoY (%) - more negative is worse
items.rationale: China's National Bureau of Statistics published on June 11, 2026 that PPI increased 3.9% year-on-year in May 2026, confirmed by TradingEconomics citing the same figure as the most recent data point.

items.label: Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium (x China)
items.rationale: Multiple sources from Q1–Q2 2026 cite ex-China heavy rare earth prices (dysprosium, terbium) at 4x–6x Chinese domestic benchmarks: Benchmark Minerals pegged 2025 at 4.4x with further widening forecast, Argus data cited by Reuters (Oregon Group, ~June 2026) cited 4x–5x for Dy/Tb, and InvestingNews (April 2026) cited 4–6x broadly. A mid-point of ~4.5x best represents the current consensus, though the range is wide and prices are volatile.

items.label: Export-control escalation (0 stable / 1 tightening / 2 retaliation)
items.rationale: The US MATCH Act (introduced April 2026, cleared House Foreign Affairs Committee) proposes sweeping multilateral controls targeting ASML and allied chip-tool makers to cut China off from advanced semiconductor equipment, while China retaliated with its April 2026 'Industrial Chain and Supply Chain Security' regulations and added US firms to its export control list in June 2026—constituting clear evidence of major new controls and active Chinese retaliation.

items.label: New tariff/anti-dumping actions (0 calm / 1 rising / 2 escalating)
items.rationale: Multiple active anti-dumping and countervailing duty investigations and orders against Chinese exports are ongoing as of late June 2026 — spanning van-type trailers, OCTG, solar cells, fiberglass door panels, and more — while the USTR proposed a new 12.5% Section 301 tariff on China on June 2, 2026 citing forced labor. However, a US-China trade truce extended through November 10, 2026 and China stopping short of counter-measures indicates escalation is contained, not a full trade-war cycle.

items.label: Nov-2025 pause status (0 extended / 1 expiring / 2 lapsed)
items.rationale: The November 2025 truce (suspending October 2025 rare-earth controls through Nov 10, 2026) technically remains in force, but on June 22, 2026 China added 10 US entities—including MP Materials and USA Rare Earth—to its export control list, with analysts describing this as a significant escalation that is 'testing the durability of the fragile US-China truce.' The truce is fraying rather than fully lapsed, as the blanket suspension of October 2025 controls has not yet been formally rescinded.

items.label: Rare-earth export license flow (0 flowing / 1 slowing / 2 frozen)
items.rationale: China's rare earth magnet export licensing regime as of late June 2026 is best characterized as selectively flowing but systemically restricted: general licenses were issued to a handful of major producers (JL Mag, Ningbo Yunsheng, Zhong Ke San Huan) tied to specific customers, while approvals for others remain 'documentation-intensive and unpredictable' with review times often exceeding three months; on June 22, 2026, Beijing further escalated by adding 10 U.S. firms including MP Materials to its export control entity list, effectively barring them from receiving Chinese-origin dual-use materials. The October 2025 broad extraterritorial restrictions remain suspended until November 2026 under the Xi-Trump agreement, preventing a full score of 2, but the overall framework is one of selective, politically calibrated access rather than normal trade flows.

items.label: Taiwan / cross-strait tension (0 calm / 1 elevated / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: As of late June 2026, the ISW/AEI China & Taiwan Update (June 26) documents continued PRC coast guard incursions near Kinmen and Pratas, ongoing CCG-research vessel coordination near Taiwan's contiguous zone, and a sustained PRC rhetorical pressure campaign against Japan over Taiwan contingency statements — all consistent with elevated but not crisis-level tension. The major large-scale 'Justice Mission 2025' blockade drills (Dec 2025) and the Liaoning carrier Taiwan Strait transit (April 2026) reflect a persistent pattern of elevated military signaling, but no blockade, kinetic clash, or full mobilization has been recorded.

items.label: Undersea cable incidents (0 none / 1 threatened / 2 cut)
items.rationale: As of late June 2026, no confirmed major cable cut causing widespread outages is reported in the Red Sea, Hormuz, or Baltic; the most recent confirmed incident was the PEACE cable cut in the Red Sea (~February 2026) and a Baltic cable damage (Latvia-Gotland, early January 2026), but the dominant picture is elevated threat and persistent minor incidents rather than a new, acute major outage. Elevated geopolitical risk discourse (Hormuz closure threats, Houthi activity, Baltic shadow-fleet incidents) and Meta/partners pausing 2Africa Pearls work in the Persian Gulf confirm ongoing threats at rubric level 1.

items.label: AI power-demand pressure (0 easing / 1 tight / 2 acute)
items.rationale: The IEA (April 2026) confirms data centre electricity use surged 17% in 2025 and that 'bottlenecks across energy supply chains have tightened,' with slow grid connections forcing developers toward onsite gas generation and ~20% of planned projects at risk of delays. Wood Mackenzie analyst Ben Hertz-Shargel (Fortune, March 2026) states utilities lack grid or generating capacity to keep pace, and the IEA's Electricity 2026 report flags record global grid connection queues and rising congestion-related curtailment—consistent with a persistently tight but not yet acute/emergency-level shortfall.

items.label: Data-center moratoria / backlash (0 none / 1 spreading / 2 widespread)
items.rationale: As of June 2026, the backlash has clearly crossed into 'widespread' territory: at least 12 states introduced moratorium bills, New York's legislature passed a first-in-the-nation statewide moratorium (awaiting governor's signature), signed ratepayer-protection laws took effect in Florida and Oklahoma, 20+ local moratoriums are active (Minneapolis, Denver, Baltimore, Seattle, etc.), and data center build cancellations quadrupled year-over-year to 25 in 2025 with further project stalls mounting in 2026.

items.label: Deficit timing (0 surplus / 1 balanced / 2 deficit)
items.rationale: The ICSG's April 2026 Lisbon meeting reversed its prior deficit call: it now forecasts a 96,000 mt refined copper surplus for 2026, attributed to weaker demand growth (revised down to 1.6%) and higher secondary/scrap output. This supersedes the October 2025 deficit forecast of 150,000 mt.

items.label: Drought in a chip/farm hub (0 none / 1 emerging / 2 acute)
items.rationale: Southern Taiwan's Tsengwen Reservoir hit 9.6% capacity (near 2021 record low), triggering water rationing affecting Hsinchu chipmaking hub; the US Southwest remains in a six-year drought with 43.74% of the US in drought as of June 23, 2026, forcing South Texas farmers to cease operations and delaying Taiwan's second rice crop irrigation.

items.label: Food-price trend (0 falling / 1 elevated / 2 spiking)
items.rationale: The FAO Food Price Index averaged 130.8 points in May 2026, roughly flat from an upwardly revised April level of ~131 — itself the highest since January 2023 after three consecutive monthly increases. While the May reading was essentially stable (–0.2%), the index remains elevated and on an overall rising trend since early 2026, with cereals, sugar, and meat sub-indices posting notable gains; this supports a rating of 1 (elevated and rising) rather than 0 (falling/stable) or 2 (sharply spiking).

items.label: Grid interconnection wait (months)
items.rationale: LBNL's Queued Up 2025 Edition (data through end of 2024) reports median IR-to-COD duration of 'over 4 years' for projects built in 2018–2024; a detailed third-party analysis of the same report specifies 55 months as the typical wait for projects reaching commercial operation in 2024. The LBNL source does not publish an exact month figure in its public summary text.

items.label: Key reservoir / basin stress (0 normal / 1 low / 2 critical)
items.rationale: As of June 26, 2026, Lake Mead sits at ~1,045 feet — only 10 feet above the critical 1,035-foot Hoover Dam power-loss threshold, at just 29% of capacity (47% of average for the date), with USBR June 2026 projections forecasting a further decline to ~1,016 feet by July 2027, well below the 2022 record low. The reservoir is under an active federal Shortage Condition, drought upstream is extreme/exceptional, and inflows for April–July 2026 are only 15% of average.

items.label: Major mine disruptions (0 none / 1 some / 2 cluster)
items.rationale: Freeport-McMoRan declared force majeure at Grasberg (world's 2nd largest copper mine) after a September 2025 mudslide, cutting 2026 output by ~35% with full restart pushed to 2028; compounded by disruptions at Kamoa-Kakula (DRC seismic event) and Codelco's El Teniente, driving a cluster of major outages that multiple banks (Morgan Stanley, JPMorgan, ING) forecast will produce a 330,000–600,000-tonne 2026 refined copper deficit.

items.label: Transboundary water dispute escalation (0 calm / 1 tense / 2 crisis)
items.rationale: The Indus Waters Treaty has been unilaterally suspended by India since April 2025, India's water minister declared Pakistan will not receive 'a single drop of water', and Pakistan's Defence Minister issued explicit war threats over water security as of June 22, 2026 — constituting clear open crisis/unilateral action. The GERD/Nile dispute is tense (negotiations stalled, Egypt and Sudan urging Ethiopia to abandon unilateral actions, US re-engaging in mediation) but falls at level 1, with armed conflict assessed as unlikely; however, the Indus situation dominates the composite indicator at level 2.
